content.text: is the set of techniques that keep an AI agent operating smoothly when the upstream LLM API enforces request-per-minute or token-per-minute ceilings. Without it, a burst of agent activity produces a cascade of 429 errors, stalls the pipeline, and can permanently degrade quality by causing retries that arrive out-of-order or exhaust an exponential backoff budget.
A rate limit is an API server-side policy that caps how many requests (or tokens) a client may send in a fixed window. When the cap is hit, the server returns
header signalling how long to wait. Rate limit

is the client-side logic that detects this condition and responds gracefully: queuing pending requests, sleeping for the right duration, re-dispatching in priority order, and surfacing structured errors to the caller when retries are exhausted.
LLM providers enforce two independent dimensions simultaneously:
. An agent that sends few large requests can exhaust the token budget long before the request count ceiling is reached, so effective handling must track both.
A single user request can fan out into dozens of LLM calls (tool selection, tool result summarisation, re-planning). The aggregate easily exceeds a modest API tier.
If a 429 surfaces as an unhandled exception, the user sees an opaque failure rather than a brief wait.
When all workers retry simultaneously after a 429, they generate another burst — increasing the wait rather than reducing it. Jitter breaks the synchrony.
Different models and tiers have different ceilings. A system that works in development on a small tier routinely hits limits in production on a different model.
Rate limits are a cost signal.
Consistently hitting limits is a sign the traffic pattern needs architectural changes — batching, caching, or tier upgrades — not just more retries.
requests complete with some extra latency rather than failing outright.
a token bucket or sliding window limiter shapes traffic to exactly the tier ceiling, eliminating 429s under steady load.
a queue lets interactive requests (user-facing) preempt background batch jobs.
tracking tokens consumed per request surfaces expensive prompts before they generate invoices.
parsing rate-limit response headers gives real-time insight into headroom, enabling proactive throttling before the server enforces it.
every wait or retry adds wall-clock time. A tight SLA may not tolerate a 30-second retry-after delay.
an unbounded queue accumulates request objects (including prompt text) in memory. Cap the queue depth and drop or reject excess requests early.
) diverges slightly from server-side billing. Buffer by 5–10% to avoid false confidence.

a per-process token bucket is correct for a single process but allows over-use when multiple workers share the same API key. Centralise the limiter in Redis or a sidecar for multi-process deployments.
when a service restarts after an outage, all delayed retries may fire simultaneously. Add startup jitter.
A complete rate-limit handler has three layers that work in sequence:
Before sending a request, the client checks its local token bucket. If the bucket is empty, it waits until refilled. This eliminates most 429s under steady traffic.
When a 429 arrives despite the pre-flight check (e.g., due to a burst from a sibling process), the handler reads the
header, sleeps for that duration (plus jitter), then retries.
. A smart client adjusts its local token bucket to stay in sync with the server's view, catching drift caused by concurrent workers on the same key.
The backoff formula for reactive retries is:
. It spreads retries across a window, avoiding the thundering-herd effect where all clients retry at the identical moment after a shared server-side reset.

attrs.alt: Rate limit handling architecture: AI Agent → Rate Limiter (token bucket, request queue, backoff logic, header parser) → LLM API (200 OK / 429 / 503) with monitoring panel showing metrics, alerts, and response headers

content.text: Requests flow left-to-right. The Rate Limiter is a local object (or a shared Redis-backed service) that holds the token bucket, a FIFO queue, and the backoff state for each in-flight or pending request. The monitoring panel (right) receives a copy of every response header and updates dashboards and alerts in real time.
Any production agent that calls an LLM API more than a few times per minute.
Batch-processing pipelines where many documents are processed concurrently by a pool of workers sharing one API key.
Multi-agent systems where sub-agents are spawned dynamically — the aggregate rate is unpredictable without coordination.
Integrations during API tier transitions — after upgrading a plan, the new limits may not be provisioned immediately; handling must cope with stale limits.
Any scenario where the user-facing SLA requires progress rather than failure — e.g., a long-running research workflow that must complete even if it takes longer than planned.
Read your API tier's RPM and TPM ceilings from the provider's documentation. For Anthropic, these appear on the
page in the console. Budget 80% of the ceiling to leave headroom for spikes.
Create a thin client class that wraps the SDK and intercepts all calls through a single rate-limit-aware method.
The bucket has a capacity equal to the per-minute ceiling and refills at 1 token/second (or 1/60 of capacity per second). Acquire a token before each request; if none are available, wait.
from the header first. If missing, use your backoff formula. Sleep, then re-submit the request (not a new one).
to avoid synchronised retries across concurrent workers.
. If remaining < 10% of limit, slow down proactively even if the local bucket still has capacity.
Do not retry indefinitely. After 5 attempts (or exceeding a total wait of 60 seconds), raise an exception to the caller with full context.
Count 429s, queue depth, retry count, and tokens consumed per minute. Alert when 429 rate exceeds 5%.

import time
import random
import anthropic
from anthropic import RateLimitError, APIStatusError

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def call_with_backoff(
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens: int = 1024,
    max_retries: int = 5,
    base_wait: float = 1.0,
    max_wait: float = 60.0,
) -> anthropic.types.Message:
    """Call the Anthropic API with exponential backoff on rate-limit errors."""
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            return client.messages.create(
                model=model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=messages,
            )
        except RateLimitError as e:
            if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                raise  # exhausted retries

            # Honour server's retry-after header if present
            retry_after = float(
                e.response.headers.get("retry-after", 0)
            ) if e.response else 0

            # Exponential backoff with full jitter
            computed = min(max_wait, base_wait * (2 ** attempt))
            wait = max(retry_after, computed) * random.uniform(0.5, 1.5)

            print(f"Rate limited (attempt {attempt + 1}/{max_retries}). "
                  f"Waiting {wait:.1f}s …")
            time.sleep(wait)
        except APIStatusError as e:
            if e.status_code in (500, 503) and attempt < max_retries - 1:
                wait = base_wait * (2 ** attempt) * random.uniform(0.5, 1.5)
                time.sleep(wait)
                continue
            raise

    raise RuntimeError("Unreachable")


# Usage
response = call_with_backoff(
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise quantum computing in 3 sentences."}],
    max_retries=5,
)
print(response.content[0].text)

import time
import threading
import anthropic

class TokenBucketRateLimiter:
    """
    Pre-emptive token-bucket limiter for Anthropic API requests.
    Tracks both requests-per-minute (RPM) and tokens-per-minute (TPM).
    """

    def __init__(self, rpm: int = 60, tpm: int = 100_000):
        self.rpm = rpm
        self.tpm = tpm
        self._req_tokens = float(rpm)      # available request slots
        self._tok_tokens = float(tpm)      # available token capacity
        self._last_refill = time.monotonic()
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def _refill(self) -> None:
        now = time.monotonic()
        elapsed = now - self._last_refill
        self._last_refill = now
        # Refill proportionally to elapsed time (per-second rate)
        self._req_tokens = min(self.rpm, self._req_tokens + elapsed * self.rpm / 60)
        self._tok_tokens = min(self.tpm, self._tok_tokens + elapsed * self.tpm / 60)

    def acquire(self, estimated_tokens: int = 0) -> None:
        """Block until one request slot AND estimated_tokens are available."""
        while True:
            with self._lock:
                self._refill()
                if self._req_tokens >= 1 and self._tok_tokens >= estimated_tokens:
                    self._req_tokens -= 1
                    self._tok_tokens -= estimated_tokens
                    return
            # Sleep for 100ms then retry
            time.sleep(0.1)

    def record_response(self, headers: dict) -> None:
        """Sync local bucket from server-reported remaining headroom."""
        try:
            remaining_req = int(headers.get("x-ratelimit-remaining-requests", self.rpm))
            remaining_tok = int(headers.get("x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens", self.tpm))
        except (ValueError, TypeError):
            return
        with self._lock:
            # Trust server's view when it shows less headroom than our local estimate
            self._req_tokens = min(self._req_tokens, float(remaining_req))
            self._tok_tokens = min(self._tok_tokens, float(remaining_tok))


limiter = TokenBucketRateLimiter(rpm=50, tpm=80_000)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def rate_limited_call(prompt: str, est_tokens: int = 500) -> str:
    limiter.acquire(estimated_tokens=est_tokens)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    # Feed back actual headers so local bucket stays calibrated
    if hasattr(response, "_response") and response._response:
        limiter.record_response(dict(response._response.headers))
    return response.content[0].text


result = rate_limited_call("What is a transformer model?", est_tokens=600)
print(result)
Async Rate Limiter for Concurrent Agents

import asyncio
import random
import time
import anthropic

class AsyncRateLimiter:
    """Async token bucket — safe for concurrent coroutines in one event loop."""

    def __init__(self, rpm: int = 60, tpm: int = 100_000):
        self.rpm = rpm
        self.tpm = tpm
        self._req_tokens = float(rpm)
        self._tok_tokens = float(tpm)
        self._last_refill = time.monotonic()
        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()

    async def _refill(self) -> None:
        now = time.monotonic()
        elapsed = now - self._last_refill
        self._last_refill = now
        self._req_tokens = min(self.rpm, self._req_tokens + elapsed * self.rpm / 60)
        self._tok_tokens = min(self.tpm, self._tok_tokens + elapsed * self.tpm / 60)

    async def acquire(self, estimated_tokens: int = 0) -> None:
        while True:
            async with self._lock:
                await self._refill()
                if self._req_tokens >= 1 and self._tok_tokens >= estimated_tokens:
                    self._req_tokens -= 1
                    self._tok_tokens -= estimated_tokens
                    return
            await asyncio.sleep(0.1)


_limiter = AsyncRateLimiter(rpm=50, tpm=80_000)
_client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

async def agent_call(task: str, max_retries: int = 4) -> str:
    """Rate-limited async call with exponential backoff."""
    await _limiter.acquire(estimated_tokens=600)
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            response = await _client.messages.create(
                model="claude-opus-4-5",
                max_tokens=512,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
            )
            return response.content[0].text
        except anthropic.RateLimitError as e:
            if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                raise
            retry_after = float(
                e.response.headers.get("retry-after", 0)
            ) if e.response else 0
            wait = max(retry_after, 1.0 * (2 ** attempt)) * random.uniform(0.5, 1.5)
            await asyncio.sleep(wait)
    return ""


async def main() -> None:
    tasks = [
        "Explain a transformer in one sentence.",
        "What is gradient descent?",
        "Define an embedding vector.",
    ]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[agent_call(t) for t in tasks])
    for task, result in zip(tasks, results):
        print(f"Q: {task}\nA: {result[:120]}\n")


asyncio.run(main())

Scenario: a document-processing pipeline ingests 500 research PDFs overnight. Each document triggers 4 LLM calls (extract metadata, summarise, generate tags, evaluate quality). The API key is on an Anthropic Tier 2 plan: 1,000 RPM and 80,000 TPM. Running 500 × 4 = 2,000 calls without throttling would trigger 429s within the first two minutes.

import asyncio
import random
import time
import anthropic

class DocumentProcessor:
    """
    Processes a batch of documents against the Anthropic API,
    respecting tier limits via a token bucket + backoff strategy.
    """
    RPM_LIMIT = 900          # 90% of Tier 2 ceiling (1000 RPM)
    TPM_LIMIT = 72_000       # 90% of Tier 2 ceiling (80k TPM)
    TOKENS_PER_DOC_CALL = 1_200   # estimated per LLM call

    def __init__(self, concurrency: int = 10):
        self.client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
        self._semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)
        self._req_tokens = float(self.RPM_LIMIT)
        self._tok_tokens = float(self.TPM_LIMIT)
        self._last_refill = time.monotonic()
        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()
        self.stats = {"ok": 0, "retries": 0, "errors": 0}

    async def _refill(self) -> None:
        now = time.monotonic()
        elapsed = now - self._last_refill
        self._last_refill = now
        self._req_tokens = min(self.RPM_LIMIT,
                               self._req_tokens + elapsed * self.RPM_LIMIT / 60)
        self._tok_tokens = min(self.TPM_LIMIT,
                               self._tok_tokens + elapsed * self.TPM_LIMIT / 60)

    async def _acquire(self) -> None:
        while True:
            async with self._lock:
                await self._refill()
                if (self._req_tokens >= 1 and
                        self._tok_tokens >= self.TOKENS_PER_DOC_CALL):
                    self._req_tokens -= 1
                    self._tok_tokens -= self.TOKENS_PER_DOC_CALL
                    return
            await asyncio.sleep(0.05)

    async def _call(self, prompt: str, max_retries: int = 5) -> str:
        await self._acquire()
        for attempt in range(max_retries):
            try:
                response = await self.client.messages.create(
                    model="claude-haiku-4-5",   # cheaper model for bulk processing
                    max_tokens=512,
                    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
                )
                self.stats["ok"] += 1
                return response.content[0].text
            except anthropic.RateLimitError as e:
                self.stats["retries"] += 1
                if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                    self.stats["errors"] += 1
                    raise
                retry_after = float(
                    e.response.headers.get("retry-after", 0)
                ) if e.response else 0
                wait = max(retry_after, 2.0 * (2 ** attempt)) * random.uniform(0.8, 1.2)
                await asyncio.sleep(wait)
        return ""

    async def process_document(self, doc_text: str, doc_id: str) -> dict:
        async with self._semaphore:
            metadata  = await self._call(f"Extract: title, authors, year from:\n{doc_text[:500]}")
            summary   = await self._call(f"Summarise in 3 sentences:\n{doc_text[:2000]}")
            tags      = await self._call(f"List 5 keyword tags for:\n{doc_text[:500]}")
            quality   = await self._call(f"Rate quality 1-10 with one reason:\n{doc_text[:500]}")
            return {"id": doc_id, "metadata": metadata, "summary": summary,
                    "tags": tags, "quality": quality}

    async def run(self, documents: list[tuple[str, str]]) -> list[dict]:
        results = await asyncio.gather(
            *[self.process_document(text, did) for text, did in documents],
            return_exceptions=True,
        )
        print(f"Done. Stats: {self.stats}")
        return [r for r in results if not isinstance(r, Exception)]


# Example usage (with placeholder documents)
async def demo():
    docs = [(f"Document {i} about machine learning …", f"doc-{i}")
            for i in range(20)]
    processor = DocumentProcessor(concurrency=10)
    results = await processor.run(docs)
    print(f"Processed {len(results)} documents successfully")

asyncio.run(demo())
Sending the same request again without any wait just generates another 429 and wastes the retry budget.
A computed backoff may be far shorter than the server's rate-limit reset window. Always take
Pure exponential backoff without randomness causes all workers sharing an API key to retry at the same instant, generating a thundering herd.
Five workers each with a 60-RPM bucket collectively send 300 RPM on a 60-RPM key. Centralise the bucket in Redis or use a sidecar.
A 500 is a server error — the request likely reached the model and may have partial state. A 429 is a policy response — the request was never processed. They need different retry semantics.
You can be far under the RPM ceiling but still get 429s if a few very long prompts exhaust the TPM limit. Always count and track tokens, not just requests.
spreads load more uniformly than multiplying by
when many workers collide at once.
Configure the local rate limiter to 80% of the documented ceiling to absorb estimation error and concurrent workers you don't fully control.
Put user-facing requests in a high-priority queue and background batch jobs in a low-priority queue. Serve the high queue first when both are waiting.
Use the cheapest capable model for bulk tasks.
Routing bulk processing to claude-haiku-4-5 reduces both cost and token usage, leaving TPM headroom for interactive claude-opus-4-5 calls.

If multiple agents generate the same or similar prompt (e.g., a shared system-prompt prefix), use prompt caching to reduce net token consumption. Anthropic Prompt Caching charges only 10% of the normal input-token rate for cached prefixes.
Include the model, estimated token count, RPM/TPM headroom at time of call, and the retry-after value. This is the diagnostic data needed to tune the rate limiter.
Test rate limit handling in CI.
Inject a mock 429 response on the first call in unit tests to verify the retry path runs correctly and that the call eventually succeeds.
A single-process async token bucket (as shown above) handles tens of concurrent coroutines efficiently because the bucket state is accessed under
and refill is O(1). The main scalability constraint appears when multiple processes or hosts share a single API key.
use a Lua script inside Redis to implement the token bucket atomically. Libraries such as
(Python) provide this out of the box with multiple storage backends.

most LLM APIs use a fixed 60-second window that resets on a clock boundary. A sliding-window limiter on the client side is more conservative (never allows more than N requests in any 60-second span) and eliminates cliff-edge 429s at window resets.

for agents that issue many short prompts, batch them into a single multi-turn conversation or use the Batch API (Anthropic Message Batches) which processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost and does not count against synchronous rate limits.

automate a PagerDuty alert or Slack message when the 7-day 429 rate exceeds 1%. That is the signal to request a rate-limit increase from the provider — typically granted within 24 hours with justification.
Python rate limiting with Redis/memcached/memory storage
Composable retry conditions; integrates with asyncio
Unified LLM proxy with built-in rate limiting, load balancing, and fallbacks
Handles multiple API keys and routing via a configuration file
Handles 429/529 with backoff; does not pre-emptively throttle
Exempt from synchronous RPM/TPM limits; results delivered via polling
Local counter, refill at fixed rate
Adds unpredictable latency; thundering herd without jitter
No pre-emptive throttle, no queue, no metrics
Sidecar with per-key limits, load balancing
Extra infrastructure; adds ~5 ms latency per call
Not suitable for interactive, latency-sensitive calls
Avoid logging full prompts in retry logs.
Retry context records may land in log aggregators accessible to many engineers. Log only the request ID, model, and token estimate — not the prompt content.
An upstream service that calls your agent without limits can cause your agent to exhaust the API quota for all downstream users. Apply your own ingress rate limiting before forwarding to the LLM.
Treat retry-after as an upper bound, not exact.
The server's quota window may reset slightly before or after the stated time. Wait slightly longer (add 500 ms) to avoid sending too early.
If 429s persist for more than N consecutive attempts, open a circuit breaker and fail fast for a fixed duration rather than tying up threads in long backoff sleeps.
Rotate API keys under independent limits.
Anthropic supports multiple API keys per organisation; assign separate keys to separate services so a batch job exhausting its key does not block interactive users.
set to 80% of your tier ceiling. Reactive backoff alone is not sufficient for predictable throughput.
to avoid retrying before the server's window resets.
Add full jitter to every backoff
to prevent concurrent workers from forming a retry thundering herd after a shared rate-limit event.
Token exhaustion hits before request exhaustion for long-context or many-step agent workloads.
(Redis or sidecar) when more than one process shares an API key. Per-process buckets multiply the effective traffic by the worker count.
Use the Batch API for offline, high-volume work
— it bypasses synchronous rate limits and costs 50% less than synchronous calls.
is a signal to request a tier upgrade, add caching, or route bulk work to the Batch API — not just to increase the retry count.
